description: For one claim or set of claims to support another by necessity: the first cannot be true in the stated interpretation while the second is false. Entailment concerns the relation between propositions, not what caused an event, what usually follows in time, what a speaker finds persuasive, or what anyone currently knows.
sound_symbolism: The grounded necessity of 'na' establishes a relation stronger than ordinary support. Deliberate 'the' asks the speaker to attend to scope and interpretation, while bright 'ri' carries the unavoidable consequence into focus.
grammatical_notes: A transitive verb with the entailing claim as subject and the entailed claim as object: 'ha mothare ha remoluma natheri.' (This premise entails this conclusion.) The event-noun rule supplies entailment. Distinct from 'ka' (cause), 'thelao' (consequence in discourse), and 'panoru' (support), all of which may hold without necessity.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Precise entailment makes the conditions and interpretation of necessity visible rather than turning a forceful intuition into an absolute claim.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Participants can distinguish a necessary relation from persuasion, status, or insistence, reducing pressure disguised as logic.

tags.cognition: a necessary truth-preserving relation between propositions
